---
title: "Battery Drain Experiment — Project Proposal"
author: "Hazim bin Mohd Izani"
date: "Spring 2026"
output:
  pdf_document:
    latex_engine: xelatex
geometry: margin=0.75in
fontsize: 11pt
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{enumitem}
  - \setlist{nosep, leftmargin=*}
  - \usepackage{titlesec}
  - \titlespacing*{\subsection}{0pt}{6pt}{3pt}
  - \setlength{\parskip}{4pt}
  - \setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
---

## Experiment Description

I will investigate how four common iPhone settings affect **battery drain rate**. Smartphones are integral to daily life, and understanding which settings most impact battery consumption has practical value. By systematically varying brightness, WiFi connectivity, app workload, and Low Power Mode, I can quantify main effects and interactions between these factors.

## Response Variable

**Battery percentage drop (%)** over a fixed 15-minute interval. Each run begins by recording the current battery level, applying the treatment settings, waiting 15 minutes, and recording the final level. The response is the difference (start % $-$ end %).

## Factors and Levels

| Factor | Low ($-$) | High ($+$) |
|:-------|:----------|:-----------|
| A: Screen Brightness | 25% | 100% |
| B: WiFi | Off (Airplane mode) | On (connected) |
| C: App Workload | Idle (home screen, no apps) | Active (local video on loop) |
| D: Low Power Mode | On | Off |

All four factors are independently controllable with no logical conflicts. Factor C uses a pre-downloaded video (not streaming), so it does not depend on WiFi status.

## Statistical Design

I will use a **$2^4$ full factorial design** (Chapter 4) with $n = 3$ replicates per treatment combination, giving $16 \times 3 = 48$ total runs. Each run takes 15 minutes, so the experiment is feasible over 2--3 days. Runs will be randomized within each day, with **Day** as a blocking factor to account for day-to-day variation.

## Planned Statistical Analysis

- Full factorial ANOVA with all main effects and two-factor interactions, plus Day as a blocking factor
- Half-normal plot of estimated effects to identify significant factors
- Interaction plots for significant two-factor interactions
- Residual diagnostics: normal Q-Q plot, residuals vs. fitted values, and residuals vs. run order to check normality, constant variance, and independence
- Multiple comparisons (Tukey or Bonferroni) if needed
- Location-dispersion modeling using the 3 replicates per cell to examine whether certain settings also affect variability in battery drain
